> **Goal:** master the single algorithm that trains almost every machine-learning
> model — **gradient descent** — and the practical knobs (learning rate, feature
> scaling, batching, momentum) that make it work.

## Why a mathematician should care

Training a model means *minimizing* a loss function $J(\theta)$ over its
parameters $\theta$. In Chapter 06 you minimized one-variable functions and in
Chapter 07 you met the **gradient** $\nabla J$, the vector pointing uphill. This
chapter turns those ideas into an algorithm: stand on the loss surface, step
downhill, repeat. Logistic regression, neural networks, and deep learning are all
this one loop with a different $J$.

## What is in the notebook

`12_gradient_descent.ipynb`, built with only NumPy and Matplotlib. Run each code
cell with **Shift + Enter**.

## Core ideas in this chapter

### The update rule
$$\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta\,\nabla J(\theta)$$
- $J(\theta)$ = the **cost / loss** (how wrong the model is; smaller is better).
- $\nabla J$ = the gradient, pointing **uphill**; we step the opposite way.
- $\eta$ = the **learning rate** (step size). We run it on a 1D bowl and plot the
  path of iterates and the loss-vs-iteration curve.

### The learning rate $\eta$
- **too small** → painfully slow; **good** → smooth, fast; **too large** →
  overshoots and **diverges**.
- For the quadratic $J=(\theta-3)^2$, the error is multiplied by $|1-2\eta|$ each
  step, so descent is stable exactly when $0 < \eta < 1$.

### Linear regression from scratch
Mean squared error cost
$$J(w,b)=\frac1n\sum_i (w x_i + b - y_i)^2,\quad
\nabla J = \frac{2}{n}X^\top(X\theta - y).$$
- Gradient descent converges to the **closed-form** least-squares solution
  (the **normal equation** $\theta^\star=(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top y$), confirming it
  finds the right answer without the formula.

### Feature scaling / normalization
- Features on different scales make the cost contours long, thin **ellipses**;
  descent zig-zags and crawls.
- **Standardizing** (subtract mean, divide by std) makes contours nearly
  **circular**, so descent heads straight to the minimum. Always scale first.

### Batch vs stochastic vs mini-batch
- **Batch** — exact gradient over all $n$ points: smooth but expensive.
- **Stochastic (SGD)** — one random point per step: cheap, fast, noisy.
- **Mini-batch** — a small random batch (e.g. 16): the practical default used in
  all modern training. We overlay their loss curves.

### Momentum (optional)
$$v \leftarrow \beta v + \nabla J(\theta),\qquad \theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta v.$$
- Adds inertia (a heavy ball rolling downhill) to accelerate through shallow
  valleys. The seed of optimizers like Adam.

## Key things that surprise newcomers

| Thing | Note |
|-------|------|
| Step size shrinks itself | steps are big when steep, small near the minimum |
| `=` is an update | $\theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta\nabla J$ overwrites $\theta$ |
| Learning rate | too big → diverges; too small → crawls |
| Feature scales | unscaled features force a tiny $\eta$ and slow descent |
| SGD noise | the loss curve jitters — that is expected, even useful |

## Building the notebook

This notebook is generated from a Python script (never hand-edited JSON):

```

```

## After this chapter

You can minimize any differentiable loss by gradient descent and tune it to
converge fast. Next we apply this engine to classification and beyond.
